verificationNote: Service model definitions based on NIST SP 800-145 and CSA Cloud Controls Matrix v4. Service examples from public CSP documentation. Risk profiles from CSA Top Threats to Cloud Computing (2024) and real-world incident analysis.
description: Comparison of security characteristics across IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, and FaaS (serverless) service models. Each model presents a different security scope, risk profile, and control surface. Understanding these differences is essential for selecting appropriate controls and assigning responsibility.

serviceModels.name: Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)
serviceModels.description: Virtualised compute, storage, and networking resources provisioned on demand. The customer has full control over the operating system and everything above it. Closest to traditional on-premises infrastructure but with CSP-managed hardware.
serviceModels.securityScope: Customer manages: OS, middleware, runtime, application, data, identity, network configuration. CSP manages: physical infrastructure, hypervisor, hardware.
keyRisks: Unpatched operating systems and middleware — customer bears full patching responsibility
Misconfigured security groups and network ACLs exposing services to the internet
Overly permissive IAM roles attached to VM instances (instance metadata exploitation)
Publicly accessible storage buckets with sensitive data
Lack of encryption at rest on EBS volumes or managed disks
SSH/RDP keys not rotated or shared across teams
Snapshot and AMI/image sprawl containing sensitive data or credentials
keyControls: Automated OS patching (AWS Systems Manager, Azure Update Manager)
Security group least-privilege review (deny-all default, explicit permit)
IMDSv2 enforcement to prevent SSRF-based credential theft (AWS)
Encryption at rest for all volumes and storage (KMS-managed keys)
CIS Benchmark hardening for all OS images
CSPM continuous scanning for misconfigurations (Prisma Cloud, Wiz, Defender for Cloud)
Host-based IDS/EDR on all VMs
serviceModels.complianceConsiderations: Full responsibility for meeting regulatory controls at OS level and above. PCI DSS, HIPAA, and SOC 2 auditors will expect evidence of OS hardening, patching cadence, access controls, and logging — the CSP's SOC 2 report covers only the infrastructure layer. NACSA NCII entities using IaaS must demonstrate all s18 data security measures independently.

serviceModels.name: Platform as a Service (PaaS)
serviceModels.description: Managed application hosting platforms where the CSP manages the OS, middleware, and runtime. The customer deploys application code and manages data. Reduces operational overhead but limits control over underlying infrastructure security.
serviceModels.securityScope: Customer manages: application code, data, identity, API security, database access policies. CSP manages: OS, middleware, runtime, platform patching, platform availability.
keyRisks: Application-level vulnerabilities (SQLi, XSS, SSRF, broken authentication)
Insecure API endpoints without authentication or rate limiting
Overly permissive database access policies (public endpoints, weak authentication)
Dependency vulnerabilities in application libraries (Log4Shell, Spring4Shell)
CI/CD pipeline compromise injecting malicious code into deployments
Secrets hardcoded in application code or environment variables
Insufficient application-level logging missing security-relevant events
keyControls: SAST/DAST scanning in CI/CD pipeline
Software Composition Analysis (SCA) for dependency vulnerabilities
WAF in front of all web-facing PaaS services
Database network isolation (private endpoints, no public access)
Secrets management (AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, Google Secret Manager)
Application-level logging to centralised SIEM
API gateway with authentication, authorization, and rate limiting
serviceModels.complianceConsiderations: Customer remains responsible for application security testing, data encryption configuration, and access control. CSP provides platform-level compliance certifications. For BNM RMiT, financial institutions must demonstrate application security controls and secure development lifecycle regardless of PaaS usage.

serviceModels.name: Software as a Service (SaaS)
serviceModels.description: Fully managed applications consumed as a service. The CSP manages the entire stack — infrastructure, platform, and application. The customer manages only identity, access, data classification, and configuration. The smallest security surface but the most dependent on CSP security posture.
serviceModels.securityScope: Customer manages: identity governance, access control, data classification, sharing permissions, endpoint security, audit log review. CSP manages: everything else.
keyRisks: Weak identity controls — no MFA enforcement, shared accounts, stale permissions
Oversharing — documents, dashboards, or datasets shared publicly or with excessive audiences
Shadow SaaS — business units adopting SaaS without IT/security approval or oversight
Data exfiltration through legitimate API access (insider threat, compromised credentials)
Inadequate offboarding — former employees retaining access to SaaS applications
CSP supply chain compromise affecting all tenants (SolarWinds, Codecov)
Lack of audit log monitoring — security events undetected for months
keyControls: SSO integration with enterprise IdP (Entra ID, Okta, Google Workspace)
MFA enforcement for all users — no exceptions
SCIM-based automated provisioning and deprovisioning
CASB (Cloud Access Security Broker) for shadow SaaS discovery and DLP
SaaS Security Posture Management (SSPM) for configuration drift detection
Regular access reviews (quarterly minimum)
Audit log forwarding to enterprise SIEM
serviceModels.complianceConsiderations: Customer must verify CSP compliance certifications (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, CSA STAR) and ensure they cover the specific service in use. Data residency requirements (PDPA, BNM RMiT) must be contractually enforced — SaaS data may be processed in any region unless restricted. NACSA NCII entities must ensure SaaS providers meet s18 data security expectations through contractual and technical controls.

serviceModels.name: Function as a Service / Serverless
serviceModels.description: Event-driven compute where the customer deploys individual functions that execute in response to triggers. The CSP manages all infrastructure, scaling, and runtime. The attack surface shifts entirely to application logic, IAM permissions, and event source configuration. No servers to patch but new attack vectors emerge.
serviceModels.securityScope: Customer manages: function code, IAM permissions, event source configuration, API security, data handling. CSP manages: runtime, OS, scaling, isolation, cold start security.
keyRisks: Overprivileged function execution roles (Lambda role with admin access)
Event injection — malicious payloads in event sources (S3, SQS, API Gateway) triggering vulnerable functions
Insecure function-to-function communication without authentication
Secrets embedded in function environment variables without encryption
Dependency confusion attacks in function deployment packages
Cold start credential caching enabling cross-invocation data leakage
Insufficient logging — serverless functions may not log security events by default
keyControls: Least-privilege IAM roles per function (one role per function, scoped to exact resources)
Input validation on all event sources — treat every trigger as untrusted
Secrets Manager integration for all credentials (never environment variables)
Function code scanning in CI/CD (SAST, dependency scanning)
API Gateway with authentication and request validation
Distributed tracing (AWS X-Ray, Azure Application Insights) for security monitoring
VPC integration for functions accessing private resources
serviceModels.complianceConsiderations: Serverless eliminates OS-level compliance requirements but introduces new challenges: ephemeral execution makes forensics difficult, function-level logging must be explicitly configured, and IAM complexity increases audit scope significantly. BNM RMiT requires financial institutions to demonstrate control over all compute environments including serverless.
